edges.description: HPV-driven checkpoint loss converges on malignant squamous proliferation and local invasion.
edges.target: Invasive Squamous Cell Proliferation and Nodal Spread

edges.description: HPV status stratifies tumor mutational burden and immune-checkpoint biomarker patterns.
Tumor suppressor loss supports malignant proliferation, invasion, and nodal dissemination.
edges.target: Invasive Squamous Cell Proliferation and Nodal Spread

evidence.reference_title: Comprehensive genomic profiling of penile squamous cell carcinoma and the impact of human papillomavirus status on immune-checkpoint inhibitor-related biomarkers.
evidence.snippet: CDKN2A mutations (0% vs. 37.5%) were exclusive to HPV16/18- tumors.

nodes.description: In the HPV-associated pathway, persistent high-risk HPV infection of penile squamous epithelium expresses the E6 and E7 oncoproteins. E6 targets p53 for ubiquitin-mediated degradation and E7 inactivates the Rb tumor suppressor, abrogating cell-cycle checkpoints and producing compensatory p16INK4a overexpression. These viral oncoproteins substitute for the somatic driver mutations seen in HPV-independent tumors.

evidence.reference_title: Different Mutational Landscapes in Human Papillomavirus-Induced and Human Papillomavirus-Independent Invasive Penile Squamous Cell Cancers.
evidence.snippet: oncogenic action of E6 and E7 substitute for mutations in HPV-induced SCC.

evidence.reference_title: Transcriptionally Active Human Papillomavirus in Male Genital Lichen Sclerosus, Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia, and Penile Squamous Cell Carcinoma.
evidence.snippet: Strong p16 positivity was a reliable surrogate marker for the detection of transcriptionally active high-risk HPV.

nodes.description: In the HPV-independent pathway, chronic inflammation (e.g., lichen sclerosus) is the background for accumulation of somatic mutations in tumor suppressor and cell-cycle genes. TP53, CDKN2A, and HRAS mutations occur almost exclusively in HPV-independent tumors, with frequent co-occurrence of TP53 and CDKN2A, driving checkpoint loss and proliferation.

biological_processes: positive regulation of cell population proliferation

evidence.reference_title: Different Mutational Landscapes in Human Papillomavirus-Induced and Human Papillomavirus-Independent Invasive Penile Squamous Cell Cancers.
evidence.snippet: mutations in TP53 (44/77; 57%), CDKN2A (35/77; 45%), and HRAS (13/77; 17%) genes occurred with one exception of a HIV positive patient exclusively in HPV-independent SCC

evidence.reference_title: Different Mutational Landscapes in Human Papillomavirus-Induced and Human Papillomavirus-Independent Invasive Penile Squamous Cell Cancers.
evidence.snippet: genetic mutations in tumor suppressor genes drive HPV-independent penile carcinogenesis

evidence.reference_title: Different Mutational Landscapes in Human Papillomavirus-Induced and Human Papillomavirus-Independent Invasive Penile Squamous Cell Cancers.
evidence.snippet: mutations in TP53 (44/77; 57%), CDKN2A (35/77; 45%), and HRAS (13/77; 17%) genes occurred with one exception of a HIV positive patient exclusively in HPV-independent SCC

nodes.description: Immune-checkpoint inhibitors such as pembrolizumab have shown efficacy in HPV-associated penile cancer and are used, often in combination, in advanced disease, motivated by PD-L1 and TMB biomarker findings.

evidence.reference_title: HPV and Penile Cancer: Epidemiology, Risk Factors, and Clinical Insights.
evidence.snippet: recent advancements in immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) have shown some efficacy in treating HPV-associated PC.

nodes.description: A substantial subset of penile cancers shows PD-L1 positivity, and high tumor mutational burden is enriched in HPV-positive tumors. These immune-checkpoint biomarkers provide a rationale for immune-checkpoint inhibitor therapy and patient stratification.

biological_processes: negative regulation of T cell mediated immunity

evidence.reference_title: Comprehensive genomic profiling of penile squamous cell carcinoma and the impact of human papillomavirus status on immune-checkpoint inhibitor-related biomarkers.
evidence.snippet: Overall, 51% of tumors were PD-L1+, 10.7% had high TMB, and 1.1% had mismatch repair-deficient (dMMR)/MSI-high status.

evidence.reference_title: Comprehensive genomic profiling of penile squamous cell carcinoma and the impact of human papillomavirus status on immune-checkpoint inhibitor-related biomarkers.
evidence.snippet: Increased tumor mutational burden is associated with HPV-positive tumors, and could serve as a biomarker for predicting therapeutic response to ICI-based therapies.

nodes.description: Both carcinogenic pathways converge on malignant squamous cell proliferation with local invasion of the glans, coronal sulcus, or foreskin, followed by stepwise lymphatic dissemination to inguinal lymph nodes. Occult inguinal nodal metastasis in clinically node-negative disease is common, making nodal staging central to prognosis.
nodes.id: Invasive Squamous Cell Proliferation and Nodal Spread

evidence.reference_title: A comprehensive review of current knowledge on penile squamous cell carcinoma.
evidence.snippet: Lymph node involvement is a common finding at first presentation and investigation of spread to deep nodes is important and can be done with the aid of PET-CT.

evidence.reference_title: Minimally Invasive Management of Inguinal Lymph Nodes in Penile Cancer: Recent Progress and Remaining Challenges.
evidence.snippet: The diagnosis of occult inguinal lymph node metastasis in clinically node-negative invasive penile squamous cell carcinoma (PSCC) has remained a challenge, with substantial perioperative complications.

evidence.reference_title: Comprehensive genomic profiling of penile squamous cell carcinoma and the impact of human papillomavirus status on immune-checkpoint inhibitor-related biomarkers.
evidence.snippet: revealed TP53 (46%), CDKN2A (26%), and PIK3CA (25%) to be the most common mutations.
